trajectory_summary: Steven arrived as a warm, self-qualified buyer with a roof-then-solar plan and a clear travel window — one of the more cooperative opening states in the corpus. Holland converted none of that into qualification baseline, cross-sell routing, or a locked appointment. The call ends with a callback promise and no qualification data captured.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag is 'unknown,' but the correct classification is 'callback scheduled — not sit-eligible.' Per schema outcomes.primary and setter_compensation_model.paid_on: sits, Holland is not paid on a callback promise; she is paid on a sit that proceeds through a full closer proposal. No verify items were captured, no bill anchor was established, and no appointment was booked. This call does not meet the sit-eligibility standard and should not be counted toward Holland's pay-period volume.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Verify execution on a pre-close lead + cross-sell bridge
primary_coaching_focus.quote: I'm gonna put a a new roof on it, and then I wanna put solar on it.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Holland had a warm, self-qualified buyer who named both a roofing need and a solar plan in the first 30 seconds. She captured neither as a verify anchor nor as a cross-sell routing signal. The call ends with zero qualification data and no roofing bridge — two compounding misses on the highest-value opening in the call. This is the single most teachable moment: show Holland what she had and what she did with it.

key_moments.quote: I'm gonna put a a new roof on it, and then I wanna put solar on it.
key_moments.stewart_read: Steven names a planned roof replacement and solar install in the same breath. This is the exact trigger condition in cross_sell_signals.roof_replacement_planned. Holland never bridges to Ion's in-house roofing capability at any point. The reactive read is that Holland simply didn't recognize the signal. There is no evidence of a strategic deferral — she never returns to roofing.

key_moments.quote: So because I'm in San Diego right now.
key_moments.stewart_read: Steven's current San Diego residence is an available proxy for bill pain, utility company, and usage patterns. San Diego Gas & Electric is among the highest-rate utilities in the country — a customer currently paying SDG&E rates is a strong bill-pain candidate. Holland could have run partial verify against the San Diego home ('What are you paying on your current bill out there?') to establish a qualification baseline and emotional anchor before the new home closes. She does not attempt it.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So I was actually gonna say so I think it would be, better if we scheduled, let's see.
key_moments.stewart_read: Holland's only scheduling move produces a callback promise, not a locked appointment. No date, time, or duration is confirmed for the actual sit. No button-up elements are executed: no one-hour block stated, no phone-appointment specification, no computer/no-driving anchor, no 'is that fair' handshake. The outcome is structurally a soft callback, not a sit.

key_moments.quote: Well, thanks, Holland. I appreciate you calling.
key_moments.stewart_read: By end of call, Holland has captured: customer name, general location (Little River), and a travel window. She has not captured: homeowner confirmation on the new property, anyone else on the title, interest-in-solar depth, utility company, average bill, roof type/age, or prior designs. That is zero of eight verify items against a required threshold of six of eight per schema verify.required_completion_threshold. This is a call-wide structural omission, not a single-moment failure.
